data.title: Trump ties Greenland threats to Nobel Peace Prize snub
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump tied his claims on Greenland to not being awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in a letter sent to Norwegian Prime Minister Jonas Gahr Støre, according to a copy obtained by Bloomberg.

In the letter, Trump wrote, “Considering your Country decided not to give me the Nobel Peace Prize for having stopped 8 Wars PLUS, I no longer feel an obligation to think purely of Peace, although it will always be predominant, but can now think about what is good and proper for the United States of America. Denmark cannot protect that land from Russia or China, and why do they have a “right of ownership” anyway? There are no written documents, it’s only that a boat landed there hundreds of years ago, but we had boats landing there, also. I have done more for NATO than any other person since its founding, and now, NATO should do something for the United States. The World is not secure unless we have Complete and Total Control of Greenland. Thank you! President DJT.”

In a statement sent to Bloomberg, Støre responded, “Regarding the Nobel Peace Prize, I have several times clearly explained to Trump what is well known, namely that it is an independent Nobel Committee, and not the Norwegian government, that awards the prize.”

Trump unsettled NATO allies over the weekend after warning he could impose tariffs on several European members of the alliance if the U.S. does not gain control of Greenland.

The European Union, on the other hand, is weighing imposing €93B ($108.2B) worth of duties on U.S. imports in response to Trump's latest tariff threat to countries that sent troops to Greenland, according to The Financial Times.

Trump also posted on Truth Social, “NATO has been telling Denmark, for 20 years, that “you have to get the Russian threat away from Greenland.” Unfortunately, Denmark has been unable to do anything about it. Now it is time, and it will be done!!!"

Europe ETFs: (VGK), (EZU), (BBEU), (DBEF), (IEUR), (FEZ), (EWU), (HEDJ), (IEV)

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.80-

data.title: Nvidia H200 chip parts suppliers pause output: report
data.description:
Suppliers of parts for Nvidia's (NVDA) H200 chips have paused production after Chinese customs officials blocked shipments of the AI processors from entering China, the Financial Times reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Nvidia had expected over 1M orders from Chinese clients, the report noted, adding that its suppliers had been operating continuously to prepare for shipping as early as March.

Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Last week it was reported that China's customs authorities told customs agents that the H200 AI chips are not allowed to enter the country. Separately, it was reported that China has started to create the purchase rules to allow domestic tech companies to begin buying the H200 graphics processing units, or GPUs.

Earlier this month, China, reportedly, told some of its tech companies to halt orders of H200 GPUs before the government decides whether to allow the chips to be sold in the country.

The H200 is the predecessor to Nvidia's current flagship Blackwell chips and the upcoming Vera Rubin. Last month, U.S. President Donald Trump approved the sale of some advanced AI chips, including the H200 GPUs to China in exchange for a 25% fee.

The Trump Administration had previously allowed Nvidia to sell a less powerful GPU, the H20, to China. However, Chinese state run media criticized the chip for lacking technological prowess, being environmentally unfriendly, and calling it unsafe. Regulators in the Asian country reportedly halted imports of the H20 over security issues.

data.title: EU seeks to bar Chinese firms from vital infrastructure: report
data.description:
The EU is looking to phase out Chinese-made equipment from critical infrastructure in the region, barring companies such as Huawei Technologies and ZTE from telecommunications networks, solar energy systems, and security scanners, the Financial Times reported, citing officials.

The EU’s cyber security proposal, which is expected to be presented on Tuesday, is expected to make an existing voluntary regime to curb or exclude high-risk vendors from their networks mandatory for EU countries, the report added.

Prior recommendations have been unevenly implemented, as several European countries continue to depend on such “high-risk” suppliers. Previously, Spain signed a €12M contract with Huawei for the Chinese company to provide the hardware to store wiretaps authorized by judges for law enforcement and the intelligence services, according to the report.

“Fragmented national solutions have proven insufficient to achieve marketwide trust and co-ordination,” said an earlier draft of the potential Cybersecurity Act, which could still change, the report noted.

The move follows growing efforts by Brussels to crack down on Chinese participation in vital EU industries.

The exact timeline for the phaseout would depend on the evaluated risk the vendor poses to the EU and the specific sector, said the officials. The proposed timelines would also take costs and the availability of alternative suppliers into account.

For example, over 90% of solar panels installed in the EU are made in China, the report added.

Some industry officials also point to the lack of feasible alternatives, because the EU has to decrease its dependency on both Chinese and U.S. suppliers simultaneously. Telecom operators, especially, have warned about the impact on consumer prices of a direct ban.

After the European Commission presents its proposal, the draft law will be discussed with the European Parliament and EU countries. The proposal could see challenges among EU lobby groups like SolarPower Europe, the solar industry body, of which Huawei is a member, due to its production of inverters that are used in solar panels, the report noted.

The European Commission and Huawei did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha. 

In November 2025, China said that a Commission push to phase out the use of Huawei and ZTE technology would violate "market principles and the rules of fair competition," the report added.

data.title: Cathie Wood says Tesla is no longer just an auto company as focus shifts to robotaxis
data.description:
Cathie Wood, Ark Invest’s CEO and CIO, suggested that Tesla (TSLA) is no longer just an auto company during an interview on CNBC’s Squawk Box. She said that while the EV sales environment has been under pressure, investor focus is increasingly shifting toward the “robotaxi opportunity.”

Asked where Tesla stands today, Wood said, “We’re moving from 15% gross margins in autos, the hardware, to more of a SAAS model where robotaxis’ recurring revenue model—those margins are more in the 70s and 80s. And I think as analysts understand that that’s where Tesla is going, and they start building in the model for robotaxis.” She added, “It’s been interesting to me to see upgrades, even by some auto analysts, now that they’re beginning to understand that this is not an auto company—maybe they’re working with their tech analysts and understanding the stock a bit better.”

CNBC also pressed on what autonomous robotaxis and robots will ultimately look like and that such ambitious efforts could take a long time to reach meaningful scale. Wood responded, saying, “We’re keeping an eye on how quickly Tesla’s footprint is expanding here, and it’s expanding a lot faster, I think, than many people expected. And it’s interesting to watch Waymo as well. We draw in Texas, we draw the footprint, and they’re competing with each other. Competition is a good thing. So I think this is actually going to happen faster than most analysts think, in terms of the proliferation of robotaxis, especially if we get more federal legislation instead of just state by state.”

Tesla (TSLA) is the largest holding in Ark Invest’s flagship ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK).

data.title: Euro Area inflation drops to 1.9% in December
data.description:
• The inflation rate in the Euro Area decreased to 1.90% in December from 2.1% last month, lower than estimates of 2%.

  • The CPI in the Euro Area increased 0.2% M/M in December 2025, rebounding from a 0.3% fall in November, matching the initial estimate.

data.title: South Korea to discuss favorable chip tariff terms with US
data.description:
South Korea will look for favorable terms for U.S. tariffs on imports of memory chips, said a presidential office spokesperson on Sunday at a televised briefing, Reuters reported.

Last year, the Asian country released a joint fact sheet on its trade deal with the U.S., which included terms ensuring South Korea will not receive unfavorable treatment from American tariffs on imported chips compared to key competitors, said the official when asked about the Trump administration's announcement imposing tariffs on AI chips, the report added.

On Saturday, South Korea's trade minister said U.S. tariffs on some advanced computing chips would have a limited impact on the companies from the Asian nation, the report noted.

Over the weekend it was reported that Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that memory chipmakers, including those from South Korea and Taiwan, could face 100% tariffs on their imports unless they boost their manufacturing capabilities in the U.S.

 South Korean companies Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F) are among the largest memory chipmaking companies in the world. Among the several memory products the companies make are high-bandwidth memory, or HBM, chips, which are vital to make advanced accelerators for AI. SK hynix is a major supplier of HBM chips to Nvidia (NVDA).

data.title: Europe’s biggest carmakers shares fall on Trump’s tariff threat over Greenland
data.description:
Shares of some of Europe’s biggest carmakers fell on Monday morning, following U.S. President Donald Trump’s latest tariff threats on several European countries over Greenland.

Europe’s Stoxx Automobiles and Parts index traded over 2% lower at around 9:30 a.m. Central European Time.

Germany’s Volkswagen, BMW, and Mercedes-Benz Group were trading between 2.5% to 4% lower shortly after the opening bell. Milan-listed shares of Stellantis and Ferrari were also trading lower.

This comes after Trump on Saturday threatened up to 25% of tariffs against eight European countries, including the U.K., France, Germany, and Denmark, which have deployed military in Greenland at a time when the U.S. is aiming to take ownership of the Arctic Island.

According to Trump, the countries, some of which are among the closest allies of the U.S., face a 10% tariff from Feb. 1, and from June 1, the levies will rise to a 25% rate unless an agreement is reached before then.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) also dropped on Monday after Trump's tariff threats.

The European Union is reportedly also weighing imposing €93B ($108.2B) worth of duties on U.S. imports in response to the tariff threats.

data.title: Tesla set to be early winner of Canada-China EV tariff deal - report
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) will likely be one of the first automakers to benefit from Canada's move to slash tariffs on Chinese-made electric vehicles, Reuters reported.

Canada will allow 49,000 EVs to be imported from China annually with a 6.1% tariff, down from the 100% rate that was in place since 2024, in exchange for tariff concessions on canola and other Canadian goods. The EV quota could rise to 70,000 within five years.

While Chinese EV makers don't have a significant sales presence in Canada, Tesla (TSLA) has an existing network of 39 stores in the country.

Tesla's (TSLA) biggest plant is in Shanghai, which is already equipped to build and export a Canada-specific version of its Model Y, according to the report.

It started shipping this version to Canada in 2023 but was forced to stop in 2024 after Ottawa imposed 100% tariffs on Chinese-made EVs. It currently ships Model Ys produced in Berlin to Canada.

To note, half of Canada's EV quota under the trade deal will be reserved for vehicles priced under C$35,000 ($25,192). Tesla (TSLA) model prices are above that number.

Low-cost cars from China's BYD (BYDDF) could benefit from that quota, but the company currently has negligible sales in Canada. 

Other automakers that exported Chinese-made cars to Canada before the imposition of 100% tariffs include Volvo (VLVLY) and Polestar (PSNY), which are both owned by Chinese conglomerate Geely (GELYF).

data.title: Prediction markets odds above 50% that Kevin Warsh likely to be next Fed chair
data.description:
Kevin Warsh is the clear favorite to become the next chair of the U.S. Federal Reserve, according to betting markets, with implied probabilities clustered in the mid-to-high 50% range.

On Kalshi, Warsh is priced at 55%, while Polymarket places his odds at 56% and Gemini at 57%, giving him an aggregated probability of about 56%, data show.

Rick Rieder ranks a distant second, with odds near 20% across platforms, followed by Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller at roughly 13–14%. Rieder has seen a sharp pickup in momentum, rising 5.6% in the last 24 hours.

Kevin Hassett trails the field with probabilities around 6–7%. President Donald Trump on Friday said he wants to keep former favorite Kevin Hassett as director of the White House National Economic Council.

data.title: EU said to weigh €93B tariffs on U.S. in response to Trump's Greenland threat
data.description:
The European Union is weighing imposing €93B ($108.2B) worth of duties on U.S. imports in response to President Donald Trump's latest tariff threat to countries that sent troops to Greenland, The Financial Times reported.

The tariffs had been prepared last year, but were suspended till Feb. 6 under the EU's trade deal with the U.S. The EU's 27 ambassadors discussed reactivating the tariffs on Sunday, officials involved in the talks told FT.

They also discussed the bloc's "anti-coercion instrument," also called the trade bazooka, which can limit the access of U.S. companies to the internal market.

France, which has backed using the ACI, and Germany are coordinating a joint response. Their finance ministers will discuss the matter in Berlin on Monday and then travel to Brussels for a meeting with their European counterparts.

But most EU member states are calling for dialogue with Trump before threatening retaliation. "We need to get the temperature down," an EU diplomat told the FT.

The proposed retaliation measures are aimed at giving EU leaders leverage when they meet Trump at the World Economic Forum in Davos this week.

"In 2025, Europe generally avoided retaliatory measures, recognizing the harm tariffs can do to the domestic economy," said UBS' Paul Donovan. "Targeted measures, including the restricting of U.S. social media providers and other services, are now more likely."
